message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: ADORE is NOT SOTA on DeepResearch Bench, and 52.65 is not the number to beat. ADORE does not appear on EITHER official leaderboard (legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro or current GPT-5.5). Its '#1' claim was true only against a ~Jan-2026 leaderboard snapshot that is now badly stale.
DETAIL: I pulled the leaderboard's actual source-of-truth CSVs from the HF Space repo (not the rendered page). ADORE's paper Table 2 lists its competitors as Tavily 52.44, ThinkDepthAI 52.43, CellCog 51.94, Salesforce Air 50.65, LangChain(GPT-5) 50.60 — those rows match the LEGACY leaderboard exactly (tavily-research 52.44, thinkdepthai 52.43, salesforce-air 50.65, gensee-search-gpt-5 50.60), confirming ADORE benchmarked against a legacy-judge snapshot. But on the CURRENT legacy board, 21 systems now sit ABOVE 52.65 (qianfan 58.03, ZTE-Nebula 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu 57.06, xiaoyi 57.00, WhaleCloud 56.81, cellcog-max 56.67, 1688AILab 56.53, octen 56.31, grep-v5 56.23, nvidia-aiq 55.95, ... RecallRadar 53.19). Note CellCog was 51.94 in ADORE's snapshot and is 56.67 today — the board moved ~4-5 pts under ADORE. ADORE would rank ~#22 today. Do not plan against 0.5265 as 'SOTA'; this is exactly the 48.88 mistake repeating.
SYSTEM: ADORE
SOURCE GIVEN: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data/leaderboard.csv
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/"},{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"arXiv:2506.11763v1 [cs.CL] 13 Jun 2025 June 16, 2025","url":"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/static/papers/deepresearch-bench.pdf"},{"title":"GitHub - baidubce/qianfan-deepresearch-bench: Baidu Qianfan Deep Research · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/baidubce/qianfan-deepresearch-bench"},{"title":"Deep Research Bench Evaluation of NVIDIA AI-Q Blueprint — NVIDIA AI-Q Blueprint","url":"https://docs.nvidia.com/aiq-blueprint/1.2.1/evaluation/benchmarks/deep-research-bench.html"},{"title":"[2506.11763] DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.11763"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench - AI Wiki - Artificial Intelligence Wiki","url":"https://aiwiki.ai/wiki/DeepResearch_Bench"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents [Quick Review]","url":"https://liner.com/review/deepresearch-bench-comprehensive-benchmark-for-deep-research-agents"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench - a Hugging Face Space by muset-ai","url":"https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about ADORE in relation to the DeepResearch Bench and the RACE metric:

Qianfan-DeepResearch achieved a RACE score of 52.65 on the DeepResearch Bench, which appears to be what you're referring to. 

To provide context: RACE (Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation framework with Dynamic Weighting) is used for evaluating the quality of generated research reports, and DeepResearch Bench consists of 100 PhD-level research tasks—50 in Chinese and 50 in English—spanning 22 distinct fields.

According to the leaderboard results, Qianfan-DeepResearch ranked at the top with a score of 53.07, with a RACE score of 52.65 being one of its component scores.

Links: [{"title":"ADRA-Bank: A Modular Benchmark for Academic Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.00986"},{"title":"[2506.11763] DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.11763"},{"title":"[2601.08536] DeepResearch Bench II: Diagnosing Deep Research Agents via Rubrics from Expert Report","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.08536"},{"title":"Benchmarks/DeepResearch Bench II - USTC-CMI","url":"https://agentresearchlab.com/benchmarks/deepresearch-bench-ii/index.html"},{"title":"SurveyBench: Can LLM(-Agents) Write Academic Surveys that Align with Reader Needs?","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.03120"},{"title":"DeepResearchEval: An Automated Framework for Deep Research Task Construction and Agentic Evaluation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.09688"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/"},{"title":"ViDR: Grounding Multimodal Deep Research Reports in Source Visual Evidence","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13034"},{"title":"February 2, 2026 DeepResearch Bench II: Diagnosing Deep Research Agents via","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.08536"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about deep research agents and benchmarks. However, I did not find a specific paper or project called "ADORE" in the results. Here's what I found:

## DeepResearch Bench and Related Benchmarks

Deep Research Agents are a prominent category of LLM-based agents that autonomously orchestrate multistep web exploration, targeted retrieval, and higher-order synthesis to transform vast amounts of online information into analyst-grade, citation-rich reports.

### Key Benchmarks:

1. **DeepResearch Bench**: A benchmark consisting of 100 PhD-level research tasks, each meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields.

2. **DeepResearch Bench II**: A dataset of 132 tasks across 22 domains with 9,430 fine-grained binary rubrics constructed through a four-stage pipeline involving LLM-based extraction, self-evaluation filtering, manual cleaning, and domain-expert refinement.

### State-of-the-Art Evaluation:

DeepResearch Bench II provides a comparison of SOTA Deep Research Agents based on 9,430 expert-written rubrics across 132 research tasks.

The search results reference various related papers and benchmarks from 2025-2026, but I did not find a specific paper or agent called "ADORE" mentioned in these results. If you're looking for information about a specific "ADORE" system, you may want to provide additional context or search terms.

content.text:
Both source-of-truth CSVs pulled. Verification complete.

**Primary sources (HF Space repo, raw files, not the rendered page):**

1. `data/leaderboard.csv` (legacy / Gemini-2.5-Pro judge) — 44 rows. Verbatim head: qianfan_deepresearch_0430 58.03, ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch-V20260519 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu_deep_research 57.06, xiaoyi 57.00, WhaleCloud-DocChain 56.81, cellcog-max 56.67, 1688AILab-DeepResearch-0428 56.53, octen-deepresearch-0508 56.31, grep-v5 56.23, nvidia-aiq-nemotron-gpt52-updated 55.95, 1688AILab-0325 55.39, ms_deepresearch_gpt52mix… 55.31, drb_cellcog 55.31, deepinsight 55.24, ms_deepresearch 54.97, TrajectoryKit 54.92, onyx 54.54, deepsynth 54.22, deepdog 53.52, RecallRadar 53.19, then MindDR-V1.5 52.54.

2. `data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv` (current / GPT-5.5 judge) — only 10 rows: cellcog-max 55.78, WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon 53.51, dalpha-deepresearch 53.10, sourcery 51.17, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98, openai-deepresearch 47.84, perplexity-Research 43.05, grok-deeper-search 41.22.

**What checks out exactly:**
- No row named ADORE on **either** board. Confirmed by direct read of both CSVs.
- Counting legacy rows strictly above 52.65: exactly **21** (qianfan through RecallRadar 53.19; the next row, MindDR 52.54, is below). ADORE at 52.65 would slot at **#22**. The claim's count and the ordered list of names/scores are correct to the decimal.
- The four competitor rows the claim says ADORE benchmarked against are present in the legacy CSV verbatim: tavily-research 52.44, thinkdepthai-deepresearch 52.43, salesforce-air-deep-research 50.65, gensee-search-gpt-5 50.60. These sit near the *bottom* of the current legacy board — consistent with ADORE having frozen a stale snapshot.
- The two-board structure is real: repo root contains both `data/` and `data_gpt55/`, corroborating the judge swap.

**Caveats I could not close (do not treat these as verified):**
- I could not retrieve the ADORE paper itself. Its Table 2 contents are asserted, not confirmed by me — though the four legacy-matching rows above are strong circumstantial support.
- "CellCog was 51.94 in ADORE's snapshot" is **not** verifiable from either current CSV. 51.94 does not appear as any cellcog overall score today; it appears only as cellcog-max's *readability* sub-score in the GPT-5.5 file, which is likely coincidence. The "board moved 4-5 pts under ADORE" narrative is plausible but rests on a snapshot I cannot access.

The load-bearing operational conclusion — 52.65 is not SOTA, ADORE is on neither official board, and planning against 0.5265 would repeat the 48.88 error — is confirmed against primary source.

Sources: [legacy leaderboard.csv](https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data/leaderboard.csv), [GPT-5.5 leaderboard.csv](https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv), [repo tree](https://huggingface.co/api/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/tree/main)

input.reasoning:
Verified directly against the source-of-truth CSVs in the HF Space repo (raw files, not the rendered page).

1) LEGACY BOARD (Gemini-2.5-Pro judge), https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data/leaderboard.csv — 44 rows. No row named ADORE. Rows strictly above 52.65 number exactly 21: qianfan_deepresearch_0430 58.03, ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch-V20260519 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu_deep_research 57.06, xiaoyi 57.00, WhaleCloud-DocChain 56.81, cellcog-max 56.67, 1688AILab-DeepResearch-0428 56.53, octen-deepresearch-0508 56.31, grep-v5 56.23, nvidia-aiq-nemotron-gpt52-updated 55.95, 1688AILab-DeepResearch-0325 55.39, ms_deepresearch_gpt52mixqwen35... 55.31, drb_cellcog 55.31, deepinsight 55.24, ms_deepresearch 54.97, TrajectoryKit 54.92, onyx 54.54, deepsynth 54.22, deepdog 53.52, RecallRadar 53.19. Next row is MindDR-V1.5 52.54 (below 52.65). So ADORE at 52.65 would rank #22. The claim's count, names, and scores match to the decimal.

2) CURRENT BOARD (GPT-5.5 judge) exists as a separate directory data_gpt55/ in the repo root (root tree contains: data, data_gpt55, tabs, utils, app.py, create_leaderboard.py, ...), confirming the judge swap. https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv has only 10 rows: cellcog-max 55.78, WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon_full100_FINAL.submission 53.51, dalpha-deepresearch 53.10, sourcery 51.17, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98, openai-deepresearch 47.84, perplexity-Research 43.05, grok-deeper-search 41.22. No ADORE.

=> ADORE appears on NEITHER official leaderboard. Confirmed.

3) The stale-snapshot mechanism is corroborated: the four competitors the claim says ADORE's Table 2 lists appear verbatim in the legacy CSV — tavily-research 52.44, thinkdepthai-deepresearch 52.43, salesforce-air-deep-research 50.65, gensee-search-gpt-5 50.60 — and they now sit near the BOTTOM of that same board. That is exactly the signature of a frozen ~Jan-2026 snapshot.

CAVEATS (not verified, and should not be relied on):
(a) I could not retrieve the ADORE paper itself; its Table 2 contents are asserted by the claimant, not independently confirmed. The four legacy-matching rows are strong circumstantial support but not proof.
(b) "CellCog was 51.94 in ADORE's snapshot" is NOT verifiable from either current CSV. 51.94 does not appear as any cellcog overall score today; it appears only as cellcog-max's readability SUB-score in the GPT-5.5 file, likely coincidence. The "board moved 4-5 pts under ADORE" narrative is plausible but rests on a snapshot I cannot access.

These caveats do not affect the load-bearing conclusion, which is confirmed from primary source: 52.65 is not SOTA, ADORE is on neither official board, and 21 systems on the current legacy board beat it.
input.corrected: No correction to the core claim — it is CONFIRMED. Two sub-details are unsupported rather than wrong: (1) ADORE's paper Table 2 could not be retrieved, so its contents are unverified (though the four competitor rows it allegedly cites match the legacy CSV verbatim); (2) the "CellCog 51.94 -> 56.67" delta cannot be confirmed — 51.94 is not a cellcog overall score on either current board (it appears only as cellcog-max's readability sub-score in data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv). Sources: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data/leaderboard.csv and https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/raw/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv
